questions.question: Which darkroom safelight arrangement is correct for handling industrial X-ray film?
options: A 60 W white bulb dimmed with a cloth
A 7.5–15 W bulb behind a filter matched to the film's spectral sensitivity, at least about 4 ft from the work surface
Any red LED strip mounted directly over the loading bench
No light of any kind is permitted in a darkroom
questions.explanation: Safelights use a low-wattage bulb (typically 7.5–15 W) behind the filter specified for the film, mounted at least about 4 ft from the bench — and even then the working time must be verified by a safelight test.

questions.question: What does the fixer do, and how long should film stay in it?
options: Converts exposed grains to silver; exactly 60 seconds
Dissolves the unexposed silver halide and hardens the emulsion; about twice the clearing time
Removes residual developer odor; until the film dries
Adds density to underexposed films; five minutes per inch of thickness
questions.explanation: Fixer clears the unexposed, undeveloped halide and hardens the emulsion. The working rule is to fix for twice the clearing time — the time for the milky cloudiness to disappear.

questions.question: A radiograph that was washed for only a few minutes after fixing is most at risk of:
options: Immediate loss of all image density
Static marks appearing during drying
Staining and image fading in storage from residual fixer left in the emulsion
Increased contrast when viewed later
questions.explanation: Washing (typically 20–30 minutes in running water) removes residual thiosulfate fixer; left in the emulsion, it slowly stains and bleaches the image and destroys the archival record.

questions.question: A circumferential butt weld on 6 in. OD pipe with no internal access must be radiographed. The correct technique and minimum coverage is:
options: Elliptical double-image, two exposures at 90 degrees
Panoramic, one exposure
Double-wall exposure with single-wall viewing, at least three exposures spaced 120 degrees apart
A single tangential shot
questions.explanation: At 6 in. OD the pipe is above the roughly 3.5 in. double-image threshold, and with no internal access the choice is DWSI: source offset outside, film opposite, film-side weld interpreted, minimum three exposures at 120 degrees.

questions.question: On a small-bore weld, attempts at an elliptical shot keep failing because a heavy reinforcement and an adjacent fitting will not let the images separate. The radiographer should:
options: Use the superimposed double-wall technique with at least three exposures at 60- or 120-degree increments
Shoot one straight-through exposure and accept the overlap
Switch to a single-wall technique by cutting the pipe open
Increase kilovoltage until the ellipse appears
questions.explanation: When geometry defeats the ellipse, the superimposed DWDI technique applies: shoot straight through and make at least three exposures rotated 60 or 120 degrees so every weld portion is presented acceptably somewhere in the set.

questions.question: In a DWSI exposure, why is the source-side weld image unusable for interpretation?
options: It receives no radiation
Sitting far from the film, it is magnified and geometrically unsharp, while the film-side weld projects sharply
It is always lighter than the film-side image
The lead screens absorb its image
questions.explanation: Image sharpness depends on object-to-film distance: the source-side wall sits roughly a pipe diameter away from the film, so its image is enlarged and blurred. Only the film-side weld, in near contact with the film, is evaluated.

questions.question: The chief advantage of a panoramic exposure on a vessel girth weld is:
options: It needs no image quality indicators
The entire circumference is radiographed single-wall in one exposure instead of a series of setups
It works even when the source cannot be centered
It doubles the film density
questions.explanation: With the source centered on the axis and film wrapped around the outside, one exposure covers the full weld at single-wall quality — replacing the three or more setups an external technique would need. IQIs are still required, spaced around the circle.

questions.question: A contract requires the customer and the fabricator each to receive an original radiograph of every weld. The efficient technique is:
options: Shoot every weld twice
Load two films of equal speed in each cassette so one exposure yields two original radiographs
Photograph the finished radiograph
Load a fast and a slow film and give the customer the fast one
questions.explanation: Duplicate-record loading places two equal-speed films in one cassette; both receive the same exposure and both are originals — no second shot, no copy degradation.

questions.question: Why does a fluoroscopist wear red goggles when stepping out of the dark viewing area between inspections?
options: To filter scattered X-rays from the eyes
Because rods are nearly insensitive to deep red light, so red goggles preserve dark adaptation in lit areas
To improve color discrimination on the screen
To prevent ultraviolet damage from the fluorescent screen
questions.explanation: White light destroys dark adaptation in seconds, but rod receptors barely respond to deep red light — red goggles let the inspector work in lit areas while keeping most of the roughly 30 minutes of adaptation.

questions.question: Which features make a direct-view fluoroscopic installation acceptable for personnel protection?
options: A dark room and a warning sign only
Lead glass of sufficient lead equivalence at the viewing window, a shielded enclosure with interlocked doors, a beam shutter, and operator dosimetry
Operator distance of at least 3 feet from the screen
Reduced milliamperage during viewing
questions.explanation: Direct viewing places the inspector in line with the beam, so protection is engineered: adequate lead-equivalent viewing glass, interlocked shielded enclosure, shutter and timer control, plus dosimetry and surveys — and interlocks are never bypassed.
